meta.title: The Clever Monkey — Vocabulary Set 2
meta.subject: English Vocabulary
meta.description: Week 1–3 vocabulary focus: jungle, tricky, piece, cheese, mistake, fortune, divide, equal.

questions.question: A 'piece' of something means:
options: A small part of something larger
questions.explanation: A 'piece' is a small part of something. The cats found a large piece of cheese — just one part of it, not a whole wheel.

questions.question: 'Cheese' is a type of food made from milk.
questions.explanation: Yes! Cheese is a food made from milk. In the story, the two cats found a large piece of cheese and could not agree on how to share it.

questions.question: The math problem is very _______. It is hard to solve.
questions.hint: Hard or difficult in a clever way
questions.explanation: 'Tricky' means clever-hard — something that takes careful thought. The monkey was tricky because he fooled the cats with a clever plan.

questions.question: Finding a gold coin on the street would be a great 'fortune.' What does this mean?
questions.explanation: 'Fortune' means very good luck. Finding unexpected money is a stroke of good fortune — lucky!

questions.question: I live in a house near the _______. There are many tall trees and wild animals.
questions.explanation: A 'jungle' is a thick tropical forest full of trees and animals. The story takes place in a jungle in West Africa.

questions.question: We _______ the pizza so everyone gets an equal number of slices.
questions.explanation: 'Divide' means to cut or split into parts. 'We divide the pizza' means we cut it so everyone gets their share.

questions.question: If the two pieces of cake are 'equal,' one piece is bigger than the other.
questions.explanation: 'Equal' means the same size or amount. If two pieces are equal, they are exactly the same — neither piece is bigger.

questions.question: I wrote the wrong answer on the test. I made a _______ by accident.
questions.explanation: A 'mistake' is something that went wrong by accident. Writing the wrong answer is a mistake — you did not mean to get it wrong.

questions.question: We have an _______ number of stickers — we both get exactly four.
questions.explanation: 'Equal' means the same. If two people get exactly the same number of stickers, the amounts are equal.

questions.question: Which sentence uses 'fortune' correctly?
options: The cat found a fortune of cheese — it was very heavy.
She could not believe her good fortune when she won the prize.
I divide a fortune every day.
The jungle was a fortune of many animals.
questions.explanation: 'Good fortune' means great luck. Winning a prize unexpectedly is very lucky — a stroke of good fortune.

questions.question: Sort these Week 1–3 words by their part of speech.
questions.explanation: 'Jungle,' 'cheese,' and 'mistake' are things (nouns). 'Divide' is an action (verb). 'Tricky' and 'equal' describe things (adjectives).

questions.question: The monkey 'divided' the cheese, which means the cats got more cheese than the monkey did.
questions.explanation: No! The monkey pretended to divide it fairly, but he ate more and more each time. In the end, the monkey ate almost all of the cheese — the cats got almost nothing.

questions.question: Arrange the words to make a correct sentence:
questions.explanation: Correct sentence: 'The cats found a large piece of cheese.' This is how the story begins — the two cats discover an unexpectedly large piece of cheese.

questions.question: The opposite of 'mistake' is:
questions.explanation: The opposite of a 'mistake' (something wrong) is 'the correct answer' (something right). A mistake is an error; the correct thing is the opposite.

questions.question: 'Divide' and 'share' have similar meanings — they both involve splitting something between people.
questions.explanation: Yes! Both 'divide' and 'share' mean to split something so others can have a part. 'Divide the cheese' and 'share the cheese' both mean splitting it among the group.
